speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: We are continuing with the interview with Erika Jacoby. This is Barbara Gordon, July 11, 1994.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left off that we were leaving Wiesau. I-- I heard later that this camp commander was later reprimanded for being too soft on the prisoners and was taken back to Germany and I think was punished. Also at this point-- at this place in Wiesau, I had a cousin who was pregnant, who we hoped that she could have a baby in the camp. But she was not allowed to have a baby, and she was promised she could deliver a baby in another camp. And they took her to Gross-Rosen, which we didn't realize at that time they also had gas chamber. And she-- she was killed there. Anyway, we-- we got back. We-- we-- we got back to the railroad station. And we were taken to Reichenbach-Langenbielau. I think they were so close to each other that it was a hyphenated station. It was southeast of Breslau and a little bit east-- no, southwest of Breslau and east of the place where we had been before. At this place, probably the most difficult part of our camp experience, maybe because we had been already for awhile because our resistance was getting low or because the circumstances became much more difficult-- when we were in Auschwitz, of course, because of our naivete and our reserve yet, we somehow did not perceive it, the enormity of the problem. But when we were here already, we saw how little hope there was for our survival. We were housed in a place in Reichenbach, which were cement blocks-- made of cement blocks, barracks. But there no-- there were no beds, no platforms to sleep on. We slept on the floor, on the cement floor. There were windows there without any glass. And this was wintertime. When we arrived there, it was the day before Christmas, and the wind and the snow would blow in and would cover us, practically. We-- we did get some thin blankets. And we used to fight over the blankets. And me and my mother fought over who should cover. My mother wanted me to have both blankets, and I wanted here to have the blanket. And one day she got so mad at me that she slapped me. And so then we decided we will cover with two blankets both of us. So that was the solution. I was never hit before in my life. We had to get up about 4 o'clock in the morning and march to about 10 kilometers to the place of work, which was in Reichenbach-- no, which was in Langenbielau. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to march on the road, and we passed by civilian areas. But I don't remember, you know, being that close to them. I remember seeing houses in the background. The road usually was full of snow and ice, and it was terribly hard to walk on them. And the-- the platform, the wooden platform, would not bend. And the snow would create a ball under our-- and we could not keep our balance. We often would fall. And if we fell, that was very dangerous. We would be shot. We would not-- it was not allowed to fall. So that we would carry the weakest person always in the middle and so that we could help and carry that person. And I remember my cousin-- we took my cousin once into the middle because she just did not want to walk anymore. She wanted to just give up. And I tell her, just remember how we used to ice skate, and just think that we are ice skating now. And just remember that, and let's go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember-- she was a year older than I was, and she was kind of a pessimist. And I was always an optimist. And I said, well, you know, why don't you put on your other-- you have one more underwear. Why don't you put that on?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, I'm saving it for colder days. I said, this is the colder days. Put them on. And so that-- I-- I think there was a difference between the way I perceived. I perceived every day, if I get through every day, that's-- that means I survived. I remember-- because I was so little, I was so young, and I weighed about 60 pounds when I-- when I was liberated. I looked more like a little child. And sometimes people had pity on me, some soldiers. The soldier that gave me the onion in Wiesau, had pity on me and gave me that onion. And here too, one day they took me to measure me for a coat. And they gave me a coat to wear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Measure?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And so that-- and then some friends made, from my panties, they used my panties for a top of my pants. And from my dress they made a pair of leggings for me so that I was well protected. I-- I felt much better than most of the people had. And-- now we worked in an airplane parts factory. I'm not sure which of-- if it was a Farben factor or a Lehman factory, one of these big names that owned the factory. And it was for the-- for the German army, for the Nazi war machine. And we were to do some kind of airplane parts, which had to be always like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] scraped and then put together and tested with some pressure, put together little parts with some spring and test it whether it would hold up. And my mother became like an overseer that we did the job well. And we all worked together. We worked from 7 o'clock-- or 6 o'clock we arrived-- 6 o'clock until noon. We were not allowed to get up to go to the bathroom. And often we were in terrible pain trying to hold back. Some people found some empty cans and used it under the table, but they would be punished if they were noted. In the-- then in the afternoon we worked until 7 o'clock. At noon, we got our lunch. And this was our main meal, which was usually something that, by this time, I learned to love and looking forward to it. It was a concoction of some kind of gray material. I don't know what it was, some barley stuff in it. I remember they also have some parsnip, which to this day I could not eat anymore. The work was terrible, because anytime they could find something wrong that we did, and we would be whipped and beaten for it. And in spite of this, when we became better, we sabotaged the work and allowed many of the parts to go through, even though they did not fit. And I don't know-- I-- to this day I can't understand how we dared to do that. Now, there were also air raids in this place. And one day we were down in the factory. We weren't taken to any shelter or anything like that. And as we we're lining up for lunch after the air raid, a tall man came by. I don't know if he was a prisoner or he was a guard or he was a-- I don't know what he was. He gave me at big container of soup, just selected me out out of thousands of people and just gave me a container of soup. I-- I wasn't-- I couldn't understand it. As I said, maybe because I looked so young. At other times, I developed a skill to work with files, which were our tools, to make little charms. Just when I was very bored and when I had a little time, I-- I made those out of-- for my own fun. And one day, a German officer saw that and started yelling at me. But at the end, he said, I want that. I want to send it home for my kids. And then he gave me an order. Every day, I was to produce more and more of these things. And then other people asked me to do them, and I got a little extra food here and there. So these things helped me to survive. One day, our group, about 26 people, were asked to leave this big hall and go and work in a smaller place. This was like a little island of-- of tranquility for us. We were not supervised anymore by these very vicious German officers, but by a civilian man, a German civilian man, who was kind, who was strict but kind. And one day-- I think my story sounds like I was always given something, but I guess it was true. One day, he gave me an apple. And we figured out-- we always had our calendar-- that it was the Jewish holiday of Tu B'Shvat. And I cut that apple into 26 pieces, and we ate that apple in there. It was wonderful. This man later was replaced, and we were then taken away from there again from-- for another working place. And I was taken to a place with only men. I was the only one that was taken away from the women and worked with men. And of course, I didn't know the reason. I wasn't explained why I was. I assume because of my skill, that I did so well these things, these small parts of the airplane. And these men were older men, mostly my father's age. And I remember clearly how I sat with them at a table, about 10 of us at a table, and I was alone. And they-- we talked. I guess I talked German, because I didn't know Yiddish, and they talked Yiddish. And they asked me where I was from and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of them gave me a comb, because he made a comb from aluminum metal that he worked with. And so I had a comb, which was a wonderful thing to have. And they used to kind of taunt me and make fun of me, because I used to tell them, don't worry, that we're going to survive. And they said, and how are we going to survive? I said, God will help us, that He's going to Himself, with outstretched arms, take us out of here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they laughed. And they make fun of us. And I-- and they said, well, if we are ever going to survive, it will be because of the Russians. I said, don't put your trust into the Russians. It's God who will save us. I just had this faith. And it was wonderful for me. I'm sure that helped me survive. And these men, you know, just-- even though they-- they didn't-- you know, they did not believe what I did, they wanted to hear it from me. They wanted me to say it to them. Maybe that helped them as well. We worked until-- well, it was Passover soon-- Passover. And during Passover, we were already working just on and off because of the bombings. And on Passover, I did not eat my allotted bread. I-- I-- sometimes I got some potatoes, and I ate that. And I don't know on which day, the fifth or sixth day, I just fainted from the-- from hunger, but was able to recover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who kept the Jewish calendar?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all did. We-- we were kind of-- we had all kinds of systems that we did. We had one woman who had a prayer book, who somehow got through-- got-- was able to save a prayer book, who used to, when we marched, she used to say it. And we used to say with her our prayers. It was just amazing. You also had, of course, some problems with some of these women who were more religious than anybody, who didn't want to work on the sabbath. And-- and the daughter of our rabbi told them, you work just like the rest of us. You-- we-- even know though we are not allowed to work on Shabbat, this-- this time we all have to be the same place, and you cannot pull yourself away from the rest of us. So these things happened. Now, after Passover, soon after Passover, as I said, we were just on and off work. And one day, I got a note. Somebody smuggled a note to me. And it was from this man that I worked with, whose name was Aronowitz. And he wrote to me. Unfortunately, I don't have to note. It would be precious if I did. And he said, Erika, I think you were right. We are going to be saved. I will see you in Eretz, Israel. Don't give up. Now, this was a very trying time because the-- the Germans asked us to start digging our graves. And they told us, you dirty Jews, you're not going to escape. We're going to shoot you before we leave. And so we dug out graves. And every day, we weren't sure when will be our last day. And one day, we went to sleep. And the next morning, we got up, and there was no wake-up call. And we went up, and there were no Germans around. And it was a beautiful day, May 8, 1945. We realized that the Germans-- that the guards had left. The gates were locked. And they were electrified.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started to dig a ditch under the gate. And since I was the youngest, I got out first. And we got out, and we started walking on the road. And we started walking toward the town of Langenbielau. And in town-- in the town we saw everywhere there were white sheets out in the windows.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a beautiful day. And, you know, birds were chirping, and the flowers were out. And to walk alone and not to have to walk in line with guards around us, it was some glorious feeling. And about in the afternoon, we meet up with the Russian-- with the Soviet army. There were a bunch of drunkard soldiers with some guns and some trucks and some horses, who were walking against us. The town was deserted. There were no Germans around. In the background, I saw-- we saw Germans fleeing. I saw them carrying their bundles on their backs. And I cried. I remembered my own, and I-- human suffering is human suffering. Even though they were my enemy, I-- it was a very tragic sight. And they-- the-- the Russian army then told us to go back to the camp. And they opened the gates, and we walked back into the camp. And they made a speech. And they told us that from now on, we're going to work for them, and we're going to stay here in the camp, and they're going to feed us. And then they left. They left. And then we decided we're not going to stay in the camp. And sure enough, it was evening, and they came back. The-- the Russian soldiers came back. They came back for the girls. And it was a terrible night. We tried to avoid them. We tried to hide and escape them. And I remember jumping from one bunk bed to the other, not to be caught. And I was lucky that I wasn't caught. The next morning, we got out of the camp. And we went into town. And we looked for a house that was abandoned. And we found a very beautiful home, and we occupied it. We were 15 of us together from the same city. And my mother was the oldest. She was kind of the leader. And we got in there, and we couldn't lock the door, but we learned how to barricade it at night. And then I went out with one of my cousins, or one of my friends, to look for food. And the town was deserted. And the stores, of course-- there were all kinds of prisoners out looking for food. And they broke into the stores. And we broke into one of the bakeries. And I shoved a lot of bread into my-- we had a canvas bag of some sort, or a burlap bag that I got. And then we passed by a butcher store. And there were pigs hanging on the hook. And I took off half a pig, and carried it on my back back to the house. And my mother's saw it, and she said, good. We now have what to eat. And she cooked the pig. We religious Jews who never ate anything, and we ate it. And of course, we all got sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, too rich.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, we thought it was punishment for eating it. But we still had what to eat for a few days. Eventually, my mother had to go out and work for some Germans, some farmers, so that we could-- we could survive. Some of us went out to eat. And I was trying to find a way to get back to Hungary. We stayed in this town for many, many weeks. Now, one incident that I want to mention to you-- and unfortunately I didn't bring it over here. I have a memento from this area. I broke into a house. That was like the following day. I think it was the house of the owner of the factory where I worked. And by this time, I had so much anger in me. This was probably the only time that I expressed that anger. I went into that house with an ax, and I broke the piano and furniture and the Dutch masters on the wall. Whatever I could, I destroyed. Now, many people went into homes to rob. I did not want anything, except for three things that I took from the house. I took a white tablecloth. I took an apron. And I took a little silver cup that I still have that had the initials EF on it. And I decided that I would pretend that that belonged to my grandfather who's name was Engel Farkas. And I took that with me, and I kept it. And I really didn't understand. I was 16 by now, but I didn't understand that this was-- that I longed to establish normal life again, and these were the symbols of my home-- a white tablecloth, an apron, and a [NON-ENGLISH]. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So emotional. Do you remember what town this was? Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was in Langenbielau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Langenbielau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And as I said, there were people in the town around me, but I didn't see the people around me. And then we decided that we had to have-- they said in order to leave town-- you can't just leave town-- you had to have some kind of paper, some kind of a document. So I went into another city, which was Peterswaldau, which was nearby-- I don't know how I got there, whether we walked there or some-- and asked them to give us some kind of a document so we would go home. But there was no way to go home. We didn't know how to-- you know, they would-- we-- so my mother made an arrangement with a farmer for-- for her work, that they would have a horse and a buggy with hay. And we all-- you see, because it wasn't legal to leave town. And we got under the hay in the buggy, and they took us to the closest railroad station. And there we waited until a train came. And the train-- the trains were kind of erratic. You never know where the train would go, because it depends where there were rails.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got up-- we got on this train, all of us, and started-- we didn't know what direction the train was going. We just went. We just didn't want to stay there anymore. There were all these Russians around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, wherever we traveled, there were Russians around. And the problem was always when the Russians came in, when the train stopped and the Russians came in and asked for documents or whatever it is. We were often terrified that they would catch us and take us away. One day, there was a Russian who realized that we were Jews. And he was a Jewish man. And he started to talk to us. And he said, I will protect you. And he stayed with us the rest of the trip, and stayed at the door so that he did not allow other Russians to come in to bother us. We traveled, I think, east and then west again. We travelled east because we went to Katowice. I remember seeing Katowice. So it looks like that's where the trail-- train was going. And one day my mother-- we got off to drink something or to eat something, I don't know what. And the train started to move, and we almost lost her because she was left off. And she ran after the train, and we were able to pull her in. At another time, we arrived to a railroad station, and the train stopped, didn't go any further. And we were at this station, abandoned station, nobody there, alone. And again, we were terrified that the Russians will come. And we barricaded the door, and we tried to sleep over. The next morning, I went out into the city. I don't know which city this was. I think it was a German city. It was all bombed. And I went out, and it was desolate. And there were only-- there was only a bench and two people, two old people, on the bench, probably Germans, a woman and a man. And they were picking lice out of each other's head. That was-- it was the symbol of utter destruction. When we got back, we were able to get on the train into another direction. And eventually we ended up in Vienna and then in Bratislava, which was Pressburg. And in Pressburg, they threw us out from the-- from the train. Some Romanian soldiers threw us out. They needed a train. And we landed on the platform. And there were some other soldiers who threw us back. And we landed on the lap-- on the laps of these Romanian soldiers, again not able to do anything because our life depended on it. We finally arrived to Budapest. You know, probably some of us knew where we were going. I didn't. I mean, I just did what everybody else did. We arrived to Budapest, and we got out of the train. And we arrived to the station, which I already recognized. And we started walking towards the house where my aunt used to leave. And I-- I remember arriving there and going up to the second floor, looking for my aunt. And of course, she wasn't there. And some other people were there, who fortunately told us that my aunt was alive and she went to Miskolc, to live in Miskolc. She survived-- not her husband, but her children were with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got on the train again, and we went and arrived to Miskolc. And at the railroad station, my brother was waiting for us, my brother who disappeared on the first day when the Germans came in. And I asked him, how did you know that we were coming? He said, we don't know. We are at the railroad station every day. Everybody who survived went to the railroad station every day to see who would come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we stayed in Miskolc for awhile with my one uncle, who always-- who also survived, who had a house there. And this is where we learned, you know, later on, that my father did not survive. He was so weak, and he refused to eat not kosher meal. And they shot him. And my brother, my younger brother, died just a week before the end of the war. He also got weak and sick, and they shot him on the march, on the death march. And so I remember crying. I remember crying and trying to make peace with that. But I don't think I really dealt with it at that time, that they were no longer there. Because we kept believing that somehow people will be coming home and then some day they would appear.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
